answer:
First, we need to find the possible values of \(x\) that make the given inequality true. The inequality is true when \(x < -6\) or \(|x-30| \le 2.\)

Case 1: \(x < -6\)
Substitute \(x = -7\) (any value less than \(-6\)) into the inequality:
\[\frac{(-7-p)(-7-q)}{-7-r} \le 0\]

Multiplying both sides by \(-1,\) we get:
\[\frac{p+q+p+q}{-7-r} \ge 1\]

Since \(p < q,\) we have \(p+q < 2q,\) and since \(p+q > 0,\) we have \(p+q < 2q < 2(30-r).\) Therefore, \(p+q+p+q < 2(30-r).\)

Thus, \(31-2r > p+q.\)

Case 2: \(|x-30| \le 2\)
There are two possibilities: \(x-30 \le 2\) and \(x-30 \ge -2.\)

Case 2a: \(x-30 \le 2\)
Substitute \(x = 32\) (any value between \(30-2\) and \(30+2\)) into the inequality:
\[\frac{(32-p)(32-q)}{32-r} \le 0\]

Multiplying both sides by \(-1,\) we get:
\[\frac{p+q+p+q}{32-r} \ge 1\]

Since \(p+q > 0,\) we have \(p+q > 1,\) and since \(32-r > 31,\) we have \(p+q+p+q < 2(32-r).\) Therefore, \(33-2r > p+q.\)

Case 2b: \(x-30 \ge -2\)
Substitute \(x = 28\) (any value between \(30-2\) and \(30+2\)) into the inequality:
\[\frac{(28-p)(28-q)}{28-r} \le 0\]

Multiplying both sides by \(-1,\) we get:
\[\frac{p+q+p+q}{28-r} \ge 1\]

Since \(p+q > 0,\) we have \(p+q > 1,\) and since \(28-r > 27,\) we have \(p+q+p+q < 2(28-r).\) Therefore, \(29-2r > p+q.\)

Now, we have two inequalities: \(31-2r > p+q\) and \(29-2r > p+q.\) Since \(p+q\) is common to both inequalities, we can set \(p+q = k,\) where \(k\) is a constant. Then, we have \(31-2r > k\) and \(29-2r > k.\) Solving for \(r,\) we get \(r < \frac{31-k}{2}\) and \(r < \frac{29-k}{2}.\) Since \(\frac{31-k}{2} < \frac{29-k}{2},\) we have \(r < \frac{31-k}{2}.\)

Substituting \(k = p+q\) into the inequality \(r < \frac{31-k}{2},\) we get \(r < \frac{31-p-q}{2}.\)

Therefore, \(p+2q+3r < 31-p-q+2r.\)

Since \(r < \frac{31-p-q}{2},\) we have \(2r < 31-p-q.\)

Thus, \(p+2q+3r < 31-p-q+2r.\)

Subtracting \(2r\) from both sides, we get \(p+2q+3r < 33-p-q.\)

Subtracting \(p+q\) from both sides, we get \(2q+3r < 32.\)

Since \(q > p,\) we have \(q > 30-r,\) and since \(r < \frac{31-p-q}{2},\) we have \(r < \frac{31-30+q}{2} = \frac{q+1}{2}.\)

Thus, \(q+1 > 2r,\) and we have \(2q+3r < 32 < 2(q+\frac{1}{2}).\)

Dividing both sides by \(2,\) we get \(q+\frac{1}{2} > r+\frac{3}{2}.\)

Subtracting \(\frac{1}{2}\) from both sides, we get \(q > r+\frac{1}{2}.\)

Since \(p < q,\) we have \(p < r+\frac{1}{2}.\)

Thus, \(p+2q+3r < 2q+3(r+\frac{1}{2}) = 2q+3r+\frac{3}{2}.\)

Subtracting \(\frac{3}{2}\) from both sides, we get \(p+2q+3r < 2q+3r.\)

Therefore, \(p+2q+3r < 2q+3r.\)

Since \(q > r,\) we have \(q > r+1,\) and since \(p < q,\) we have \(p < r+1.\)

Thus, \(p+2q+3r < 2q+3(r+1) = 2q+3r+3.\)

Subtracting \(3\) from both sides, we get \(p+2q+3r < 2q+3r.\)

Since \(q > r,\) we have \(q > r+1,\) and since \(p < q,\) we have \(p < r+1.\)

Thus, \(p+2q+3r < 2q+3(r+1) = 2q+3r+3.\)

Subtracting \(3q\) from both sides, we get \(p-q < 3r+3.\)

Since \(p < q,\) we have \(p < 3r+3+q.\)

Thus, \(p+2q+3r < 2q+3r < p+3q+3r.\)

Subtracting \(3r\) from both sides, we get \(p+q < 2q.\)

Since \(p < q,\) we have \(p+q < q+q = 2q.\)

Therefore, our initial assumption that \(p+q < 2q\) is true.

Thus, \(p+2q+3r < p+2q.\)

Subtracting \(p\) from both sides, we get \(2q+3r < q.\)

Since \(q > r,\) we have \(q > r+1,\) and since \(p < q,\) we have \(p < r+1.\)

Thus, \(2q+3r < q < r+1+q = r+2q.\)

Subtracting \(2q\) from both sides, we get \(r < 0.\)

Since \(r < \frac{31-p-q}{2},\) we have \(r < \frac{31-p-q}{2} < 0,\) which is a contradiction.

Therefore, our assumption that the given inequality is true if and only if either \(x < -6\) or \(|x-30| \le 2\) is false.

Since the given assumption is false, the problem has no solution.

\boxed{No\ solution}
